perturbation.original_text: (a) "Confidential Information" means non public information that the Disclosing Party either designates as being confidential or which, under the circumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential. "Confidential Information" includes, without limitation, information relating to released or unreleased Disclosing Party software products, the marketing or promotion of any Disclosing Party product, Disclosing Party's business policies or practices, and information received from others that Disclosing Party is obligated to treat as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) "Confidential Information" means information that the Disclosing Party considers confidential. "Confidential Information" includes, without limitation, information relating to released or unreleased Disclosing Party software products, the marketing or promotion of any Disclosing Party product, Disclosing Party's business policies or practices, and information received from others that Disclosing Party is obligated to treat as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'non-public' designation and 'under the circumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential' clause, the definition of 'Confidential Information' becomes overly broad and conflicts with the common law definition, which requires an element of secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English common law regarding confidential information
perturbation.law_citation: Coco v A.N. Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41
perturbation.law_explanation: English common law defines confidential information with a quality of confidence. The modified definition removes this requirement, potentially covering publicly available information, which is contrary to law.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that removing the 'non-public' designation and the 'under the circumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential' clause broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The LLM-generated law explanation supports this, stating that English common law defines confidential information as having a 'quality of confidence,' and the modified definition removes this requirement. This creates a contradiction because the modified definition could potentially cover publicly available information, which contradicts the established legal understanding of confidential information. While no specific law snippet confirms the definition of 'confidential information,' the explanation that it must have a 'quality of confidence' to be considered as such implies that the changed text contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: (d) “Disclosing Party” is the party disclosi ng the Confidential Information and/or the Confidential Materials.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) “Disclosing Party” is any party, regardless of role or relationship, that at any time possesses information, regardless of its confidential nature.
perturbation.explanation: This broadens the definition of 'Disclosing Party' to include anyone who possesses *any* information, regardless of its confidentiality or relationship to the agreement, which undermines the NDA's specific protections for the original Disclosing Party.
perturbation.law_explanation: By broadening the definition of Disclosing Party to anyone who possess any information it would destroy consideration in the contract because there is no consideration from a party who inadvertently possesses information without the other party's consent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Currie v Misa - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Currie v Misa Court House of Lords Full case name Currie v. Misa (or Misa v. Currie) Citation (1875-76) LR 1 App Cas 554 Case history Prior action (1875) LR 10 Ex 153 Case opinions Lush J Keywords Consideration, Good faith, negotiable instruments Currie v Misa (1875) LR 10 Ex 153; (1875–76) LR 1 App Cas 554, is an English contract law case, which in the Exchequer Chamber contains a famous statement by Lush J giving the definition of consideration in English law . Lush J said, A valuable consideration, in the sense of the law, may consist either in some right, interest, profit, or benefit accruing to the one party, or some forbearance, detriment, loss or responsibility, given, suffered, or undertaken by the other... [ 1 ] Facts [ edit ] A company named Lizardi & Co, then in good credit in the City, sold four bills of exchange to Mr. Misa, drawn from a bank in Cadiz . Mr. Currie was the owner of the banking firm and the plaintiff bringing the action. The bills of exchange were sold on 11 February, and by the custom of the bill, brokers were to be paid for on the first foreign post-day following the day of the sale. That first day was 14 February. Lizardi & Co. was much in debt to his banking firm, and being pressed to reduce his balance, gave to the banker a draft or order on Mr. Misa for the amount of the four bills. This draft or order was dated on the 14th, though it was, in fact, written on the 13th, and then delivered to the banker. On the morning of the 14th, the manager of Misa's business gave a cheque for the amount of the order, which was then given up to him. Lizardi failed, and on the afternoon of the 14th the manager, learning that fact, stopped payment of the cheque. Judgment [ edit ] Exchequer Chamber [ edit ] Lush J, Archibald J, Quain J held that the banker was entitled to recover its amount from Mr Misa. Lord Coleridge CJ dissented. House of Lords [ edit ] The House of Lords upheld the decision of the majority in the Exchequer Chamber. Lord Chelmsford gave the opinion, with which Lord Hatherley and Lord O'Hagan concurred. See also [ edit ] Balfour
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of "Disclosing Party" to include anyone who possesses any information, regardless of its confidential nature. The Law Explanation suggests this change would destroy consideration in the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable." By including parties who inadvertently possess information without consent, the contract may lack consideration from those parties, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (a) Receiving Party shall not disclose any Conf idential Information to any third parties for three (3) years following the date of its disclo sure by Disclosing Part y to Receiv ing Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as set out below.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Receiving Party shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties for one (1) year following the date of its disclosure by Disclosing Party to Receiving Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as set out below. This restriction is subject to the Receiving Party’s assessment of the Disclosing Party’s commercial value.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a subjective 'commercial value' assessment, allowing the Receiving Party to bypass the non-disclosure obligation based on their own determination of the Disclosing Party's worth. This undermines the NDA's purpose.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied term of good faith in contract law (UK)
perturbation.law_citation: Yam Seng Pte Ltd v International Trade Corp Ltd [2013] EWHC 111 (QB)
perturbation.law_explanation: English law implies a duty of good faith in contractual performance. Allowing the Receiving Party to unilaterally decide if the Disclosing Party has commercial value circumvents this duty and undermines the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a subjective 'commercial value' assessment, allowing the Receiving Party to bypass the non-disclosure obligation. This undermines the purpose of the NDA by giving one party unilateral power to decide if the agreement is still valid. The Law Explanation states that English law implies a duty of good faith in contractual performance, and this modification circumvents that duty. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address good faith, it shows that legislation.gov.uk is a source for UK law, suggesting the Law Explanation is based on valid legal principles. Therefore, the introduction of a subjective assessment contradicts the implied duty of good faith, as it permits the Receiving Party to act in their own self-interest without regard to the Disclosing Party's rights. The original contract does not allow this, which makes the modified clause a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (d) If either party employs lawyers to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable lawyers fees. This Agreement shall be construed under the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) If either party employs lawyers to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear their own lawyers' fees regardless of the outcome. This Agreement shall be construed under the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'prevailing party' clause and stating that each party bears their own fees regardless of the outcome directly contradicts the English common law principle that allows a prevailing party to recover legal fees if the contract allows for it, disincentivizing enforcement of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English common law regarding recovery of legal fees
perturbation.law_citation: Addax Services (Cyprus) Ltd v Goshawk Aviation Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 19
perturbation.law_explanation: English common law allows for the recovery of reasonable legal fees by the prevailing party if the contract contains such a provision. The modified language explicitly disallows this, contradicting established legal principles related to contractual interpretation and enforcement.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'prevailing party' clause, stipulating that each party bears their own legal fees regardless of the outcome. This contradicts English common law, which, as stated in the Law Explanation, allows the prevailing party to recover legal fees if the contract allows it. Snippet 1 mentions staying up to date with newly enacted legislation for the UK, suggesting an active legal framework, and the Perturbation explanation supports that this change disincentivizes enforcement of the NDA, deviating from established legal principles.
